name: Albert Parsons
description: American labor organizer, anarchist, and editor of the anarchist newspaper The Alarm. A former Confederate soldier turned radical socialist, he was convicted in the Haymarket trial despite not being present when the bomb was thrown. He was hanged in Chicago on November 11, 1887, becoming one of the iconic martyrs of the international labor movement.

cases.charges: Conspiracy to commit murder (Haymarket bombing)

name: August Spies
description: German-American anarchist labor activist and editor of the German-language anarchist newspaper Arbeiter-Zeitung in Chicago. He spoke at the Haymarket rally on May 4, 1886, shortly before a bomb was thrown at police. Convicted on a conspiracy theory of liability, he was hanged on November 11, 1887, declaring from the gallows that 'the time will come when our silence will be more powerful than the voices you strangle today.'

cases.charges: Conspiracy to commit murder (Haymarket bombing)

name: Adolph Fischer
description: German-American anarchist typesetter at the Arbeiter-Zeitung and a leader of the International Working People's Association in Chicago. He helped organize the Haymarket rally and was convicted of conspiracy in the bombing despite a lack of evidence tying him to the bomb itself. He was hanged on November 11, 1887, alongside Parsons, Spies, and Engel.

cases.charges: Conspiracy to commit murder (Haymarket bombing)

name: George Engel
description: German-American anarchist and toy shop owner in Chicago, a prominent member of the autonomist wing of the anarchist movement. He was not present at the Haymarket rally but was convicted of conspiracy based on his revolutionary writings and meetings. He was hanged on November 11, 1887, in one of the most notorious miscarriages of justice in American legal history.

cases.charges: Conspiracy to commit murder (Haymarket bombing)

name: Louis Lingg
description: German-born anarchist carpenter and the youngest of the Haymarket defendants, known for his uncompromising revolutionary views. He was convicted of conspiracy in the Haymarket bombing despite no evidence he was present at the rally. On November 10, 1887, the day before his scheduled execution, he committed suicide in his cell by detonating a smuggled blasting cap in his mouth.

cases.charges: Conspiracy to commit murder (Haymarket bombing)
cases.sentence: Death by hanging (suicide in cell day before execution)

name: Samuel Fielden
description: English-born American socialist and anarchist teamster who was speaking at the Haymarket rally when police moved in and the bomb was thrown. He was wounded by police gunfire, then tried and sentenced to death, later commuted to life in prison by Governor Richard Oglesby. He was pardoned in 1893 by Illinois Governor John Peter Altgeld, who issued a scathing critique of the trial.

cases.charges: Conspiracy to commit murder (Haymarket bombing)
cases.sentence: Death, commuted to life; pardoned by Gov. Altgeld 1893

name: Michael Schwab
description: German-born American anarchist and assistant editor of the Arbeiter-Zeitung in Chicago. He was convicted of conspiracy in the Haymarket bombing despite being at a meeting across town when the bomb exploded. Sentenced to death and then commuted to life imprisonment, he was pardoned by Governor Altgeld in 1893 and died of tuberculosis contracted in prison.

cases.charges: Conspiracy to commit murder (Haymarket bombing)
cases.sentence: Death, commuted to life; pardoned by Gov. Altgeld 1893

name: Oscar Neebe
description: American-born anarchist of German parentage and a yeast salesman who helped organize Chicago's German-speaking labor movement. He was convicted as a Haymarket conspirator on extremely thin evidence and sentenced to 15 years in prison. He was pardoned in 1893 by Governor Altgeld along with Fielden and Schwab and later operated a saloon in Chicago.

cases.charges: Conspiracy to commit murder (Haymarket bombing)

name: John Turner
description: British anarcho-syndicalist and trade union organizer who became the first person prosecuted under the 1903 Anarchist Exclusion Act when he visited the United States on a lecture tour. He was arrested in New York in October 1903 and held on Ellis Island while his deportation case (Turner v. Williams) went to the Supreme Court. The Court upheld the act in 1904 and he was deported to England.

cases.charges: Violation of Anarchist Exclusion Act of 1903
cases.convicted: Yes — ordered deported (Turner v. Williams, 194 U.S. 279)

name: Emma Goldman
description: Russian-born anarchist, writer, and lecturer who became one of the most influential radical voices in American history, advocating free speech, birth control, women's equality, and workers' rights. She was imprisoned multiple times, most notably in 1917 with Alexander Berkman for conspiring to obstruct the draft through the No-Conscription League, serving two years at Jefferson City. She was deported to Soviet Russia aboard the USS Buford on December 21, 1919, during the Palmer Raids.

cases.charges: Conspiracy to violate Selective Service Act of 1917
cases.convicted: Yes — conspiracy to obstruct the draft
cases.sentence: 2 years imprisonment and $10,000 fine; deported to Russia December 1919

name: Alexander Berkman
description: Russian-born anarchist writer and activist, lifelong partner and comrade of Emma Goldman, best known for his 1892 attentat on industrialist Henry Clay Frick during the Homestead Strike. He served 14 years in Pennsylvania's Western Penitentiary, then was again imprisoned in 1917 for two years at Atlanta for organizing the No-Conscription League against WWI conscription. He was deported with Goldman aboard the USS Buford on December 21, 1919.

cases.charges: Attempted murder of Henry Clay Frick; aggravated assault; entering with intent to kill

name: Ricardo Flores Magón
description: Mexican anarchist, journalist, and intellectual leader of the Partido Liberal Mexicano whose newspaper Regeneración helped ignite the Mexican Revolution. Operating from exile in the United States, he was repeatedly imprisoned under the Neutrality Act and the Espionage Act for organizing armed expeditions and publishing revolutionary writings. He died on November 21, 1922, in Leavenworth federal penitentiary, where his supporters charged he was effectively murdered through medical neglect.

cases.charges: Violation of Espionage Act of 1917

name: Enrique Flores Magón
description: Mexican anarchist and revolutionary, younger brother and lifelong collaborator of Ricardo Flores Magón in the Partido Liberal Mexicano and Regeneración. He was repeatedly arrested and imprisoned in the United States, including under the Neutrality Act and Espionage Act, for organizing the PLM's revolutionary activities from exile. After Ricardo's death at Leavenworth he was released and eventually returned to Mexico where he continued political activism until his death in 1954.

cases.charges: Violation of Espionage Act of 1917

name: Marcus Garvey
description: Jamaican-born Pan-Africanist leader who founded the Universal Negro Improvement Association, the largest Black mass movement in U.S. history, and the Black Star Line shipping company. He was convicted of mail fraud in 1923 in a politically charged federal prosecution related to Black Star Line stock sales and sentenced to five years at Atlanta Penitentiary. President Calvin Coolidge commuted his sentence in 1927 and he was immediately deported to Jamaica, never returning to the United States.

cases.charges: Mail fraud (sale of Black Star Line stock)
cases.sentence: 5 years; commuted by President Coolidge and deported to Jamaica

name: Charles E. Ruthenberg
description: American socialist and founding leader of the Communist Party USA, considered the party's most important early organizer until his death in 1927. He was first imprisoned in 1917-1918 for opposing the draft and again served a year in Ohio for criminal syndicalism. After the 1922 raid on the underground party convention at Bridgman, Michigan, he was convicted under the Michigan Criminal Syndicalism Act and sentenced to up to ten years, but he died before exhausting his appeal.

cases.charges: Ohio Criminal Syndicalism Act

name: Charlotte Anita Whitney
description: Wellesley-educated philanthropist, suffragist, and niece of Supreme Court Justice Stephen Field who became a founding member of the Communist Labor Party of California. She was convicted in 1920 under California's Criminal Syndicalism Act for her party membership, a conviction the U.S. Supreme Court upheld in the landmark free-speech case Whitney v. California (1927). California Governor Clement C. Young pardoned her shortly after the Supreme Court ruling, citing her lifetime of public service.

cases.charges: California Criminal Syndicalism Act of 1919
cases.sentence: 1 to 14 years (largely on bail pending appeal); pardoned by Gov. Clement C. Young 1927

name: Angelo Herndon
description: Black communist labor organizer arrested in Atlanta in July 1932 for leading an interracial hunger march and possessing radical literature. Convicted under an 1861 Georgia slave-insurrection statute and sentenced to 18-20 years on a chain gang. The International Labor Defense made his case a cause celebre; the U.S. Supreme Court reversed his conviction in Herndon v. Lowry (1937), striking down the Georgia statute as a violation of free speech.

cases.charges: Attempting to incite insurrection under 1861 Georgia slave-revolt statute
cases.convicted: Yes — conviction reversed by SCOTUS in Herndon v. Lowry (1937)
cases.sentence: 18-20 years on a Georgia chain gang

name: Dirk DeJonge
description: Oregon communist organizer arrested for speaking at a peaceful Communist Party meeting in Portland in July 1934 protesting police shootings of striking longshoremen. Convicted under Oregon's Criminal Syndicalism Law and sentenced to seven years. The U.S. Supreme Court unanimously reversed the conviction in De Jonge v. Oregon (1937), holding that peaceable assembly for lawful discussion cannot be made a crime.

cases.charges: Violation of Oregon Criminal Syndicalism Law
cases.convicted: Yes — conviction reversed by SCOTUS in De Jonge v. Oregon (1937)

name: Haywood Patterson
description: One of the nine Black teenagers falsely accused of raping two white women on a freight train near Paint Rock, Alabama on March 25, 1931. Convicted four separate times by all-white juries despite recantation by accuser Ruby Bates. Escaped from an Alabama prison in 1948, fled to Michigan, and later died of cancer in a Michigan prison while serving time for an unrelated manslaughter conviction.

cases.charges: Rape of Victoria Price and Ruby Bates
cases.convicted: Yes — convicted four times by all-white juries

name: Clarence Norris
description: One of the Scottsboro Nine, arrested March 25, 1931 and falsely convicted of raping two white women on an Alabama freight train. Sentenced to death three times; his appeals produced two landmark Supreme Court rulings (Powell v. Alabama 1932 and Norris v. Alabama 1935). Paroled in 1946, he jumped parole and lived as a fugitive until pardoned by Alabama Governor George Wallace in October 1976. He was the last surviving Scottsboro defendant.

cases.charges: Rape of Victoria Price and Ruby Bates
cases.convicted: Yes — pardoned by Governor George Wallace in 1976

name: Charlie Weems
description: At 19 the oldest of the Scottsboro Nine, arrested March 25, 1931 in Paint Rock, Alabama on fabricated rape charges. Convicted four times by all-white Alabama juries and sentenced to 75 to 105 years. Paroled in 1943 after serving more than twelve years.

cases.charges: Rape of Victoria Price and Ruby Bates

name: Andy Wright
description: One of the Scottsboro Nine, arrested at age 19 on March 25, 1931 along with his younger brother Roy. Convicted multiple times by all-white Alabama juries on fabricated rape charges. Paroled in 1944, returned to prison after a parole violation, and finally released in 1950 — the last of the Scottsboro defendants to leave prison.

cases.charges: Rape of Victoria Price and Ruby Bates

name: Roy Wright
description: Youngest of the Scottsboro Nine, only 12 years old when arrested on March 25, 1931 with his older brother Andy. His first trial ended in a hung jury when eleven jurors demanded the death penalty for a child; charges were eventually dropped in 1937 after he had spent six years in jail. He took his own life in 1959.

cases.charges: Rape of Victoria Price and Ruby Bates
cases.convicted: No — charges dropped 1937 after 6 years in custody

name: Olen Montgomery
description: One of the Scottsboro Nine, arrested at age 17 on March 25, 1931 in Paint Rock, Alabama. Nearly blind, he was riding the freight train hoping to earn money for a pair of glasses when he was pulled off and charged with rape. Charges were dropped against him in July 1937 after six and a half years of imprisonment.

cases.charges: Rape of Victoria Price and Ruby Bates

name: Ozie Powell
description: One of the Scottsboro Nine, arrested at 16 on March 25, 1931. Lent his name to the landmark Supreme Court case Powell v. Alabama (1932), which established the right to counsel in capital cases. Shot in the head by a deputy sheriff in 1936 while shackled in a car, suffering permanent brain damage; paroled in 1946.

cases.charges: Rape of Victoria Price and Ruby Bates; later assault on a deputy
cases.sentence: 20 years for assault on deputy after rape charges dropped

name: Willie Roberson
description: One of the Scottsboro Nine, arrested at 16 on March 25, 1931. Suffering from severe syphilis and gonorrhea that made walking painful, he was physically incapable of the crime alleged. Charges were dropped in July 1937 after he had spent more than six years in Alabama jails and prisons.

cases.charges: Rape of Victoria Price and Ruby Bates

name: Eugene Williams
description: One of the Scottsboro Nine, arrested at age 13 on March 25, 1931. Initially sentenced to death, his conviction was set aside on the grounds that he had been tried as an adult while a juvenile. Charges were dropped in July 1937 after more than six years in custody.

cases.charges: Rape of Victoria Price and Ruby Bates
cases.convicted: No — conviction set aside (tried as adult while juvenile); charges dropped 1937

name: James P. Cannon
description: Founder and National Secretary of the Socialist Workers Party and one of the principal figures in American Trotskyism. Convicted in December 1941 in the first peacetime prosecution under the Smith Act, alongside other SWP members and leaders of Minneapolis Teamsters Local 544. Served 13 months of a 16-month federal sentence at Sandstone, Minnesota.

cases.charges: Seditious conspiracy and advocating overthrow of the U.S. government under the Smith Act

name: Vincent Raymond Dunne
description: Trotskyist organizer and leader of the 1934 Minneapolis Teamsters strike that helped transform American industrial unionism. A leader of Teamsters Local 544 and the Socialist Workers Party, he was convicted in the December 1941 Minneapolis Smith Act trial — the first peacetime prosecution under the new sedition law. Served 13 months of a 16-month federal sentence.

cases.charges: Seditious conspiracy under the Smith Act

name: Carl Skoglund
description: Swedish-born Trotskyist and Teamsters organizer central to the 1934 Minneapolis Teamsters strike. Convicted in the December 1941 Minneapolis Smith Act trial and sentenced to 16 months in federal prison. Faced deportation proceedings for decades after his release; he died in 1960 while still fighting deportation to Sweden.

cases.charges: Seditious conspiracy under the Smith Act
cases.sentence: 16 months federal imprisonment; later faced deportation proceedings

name: Farrell Dobbs
description: Trotskyist leader and Teamsters organizer who pioneered over-the-road truckers' organizing in the 1930s. Convicted in the December 1941 Minneapolis Smith Act trial and sentenced to 12 months and a day. Later succeeded James P. Cannon as National Secretary of the Socialist Workers Party and was the SWP's presidential candidate in four elections.

cases.charges: Seditious conspiracy under the Smith Act
cases.sentence: 12 months and a day federal imprisonment

name: Grace Carlson
description: Socialist Workers Party leader and 1940 SWP candidate for U.S. Senate from Minnesota, with a Ph.D. in educational psychology. The only woman convicted in the December 1941 Minneapolis Smith Act trial, she received a 16-month federal sentence and served at the Federal Reformatory for Women in Alderson, West Virginia.

cases.charges: Seditious conspiracy under the Smith Act

name: Felix Morrow
description: Trotskyist journalist, theorist, and author of Revolution and Counter-Revolution in Spain. Editor of the Socialist Workers Party press and a leading SWP intellectual, he was convicted in the December 1941 Minneapolis Smith Act trial and served 13 months of a 16-month federal sentence.

cases.charges: Seditious conspiracy under the Smith Act

name: Albert Goldman
description: Trotskyist lawyer who served as Leon Trotsky's American attorney and represented the defendants in the 1941 Minneapolis Smith Act trial — while himself being one of the defendants. Delivered an eight-hour closing summation widely regarded as a classic of socialist courtroom oratory. Sentenced to 16 months in federal prison.

cases.charges: Seditious conspiracy under the Smith Act

name: Max Geldman
description: Trotskyist organizer and leader of the Minneapolis Federal Workers Section of the Workers Alliance, which organized unemployed workers during the Depression. Convicted in the December 1941 Minneapolis Smith Act trial and sentenced to 16 months in federal prison.

cases.charges: Seditious conspiracy under the Smith Act

name: Oscar Coover
description: Veteran Minneapolis electrical worker and Trotskyist who was among the founding leaders of the Socialist Workers Party and a stalwart of Teamsters Local 544. Convicted in the December 1941 Minneapolis Smith Act trial and sentenced to 12 months and a day in federal prison.

cases.charges: Seditious conspiracy under the Smith Act
cases.sentence: 12 months and a day federal imprisonment

name: Harry DeBoer
description: Minneapolis Teamsters organizer wounded by police gunfire during the 1934 truckers' strike. A leader of Teamsters Local 544 and the Socialist Workers Party, he was convicted in the December 1941 Minneapolis Smith Act trial and sentenced to 16 months in federal prison.

cases.charges: Seditious conspiracy under the Smith Act

name: Henry Leyvas
description: Lead defendant in the Sleepy Lagoon murder trial, accused by Los Angeles County prosecutors of being the ringleader in the August 2, 1942 killing of José Díaz at a reservoir near Commerce, California. Convicted of first-degree murder in January 1943 by an all-white jury after a mass trial of twenty-two Mexican-American youths. The convictions were unanimously reversed by the California District Court of Appeal in October 1944 in People v. Zammora.

cases.charges: First-degree murder of José Díaz; assault
cases.convicted: Yes — convictions reversed by California District Court of Appeal in People v. Zammora (1944)

name: Manuel Reyes
description: Mexican-American youth and one of twelve defendants convicted in the January 1943 Sleepy Lagoon murder trial after the August 1942 death of José Díaz in Los Angeles County. Convicted of second-degree murder by an all-white jury. The convictions were reversed by the California District Court of Appeal in October 1944.

cases.charges: Second-degree murder of José Díaz
cases.convicted: Yes — conviction reversed in People v. Zammora (1944)

name: Robert Telles
description: Mexican-American youth convicted of second-degree murder in the mass Sleepy Lagoon trial of January 1943, following the August 1942 death of José Díaz at a Los Angeles County reservoir. The case became a focus of organizing by the Sleepy Lagoon Defense Committee. Convictions of all twelve defendants were reversed by the California District Court of Appeal in October 1944.

cases.charges: Second-degree murder of José Díaz
cases.convicted: Yes — conviction reversed in People v. Zammora (1944)

name: José Tellez
description: Mexican-American youth nicknamed Smiling who was among the twelve defendants convicted in the January 1943 Sleepy Lagoon murder trial. Charged in the August 1942 death of José Díaz, he was convicted by an all-white Los Angeles jury in a proceeding marred by the prosecution's reliance on racial pseudoscience. Conviction reversed October 1944 by the California District Court of Appeal.

cases.charges: Murder of José Díaz
cases.convicted: Yes — conviction reversed in People v. Zammora (1944)

name: Gustavo Zamora
description: Mexican-American defendant whose surname gave its name to the appellate decision that overturned the Sleepy Lagoon convictions, People v. Zammora (1944). Convicted in January 1943 of second-degree murder in the August 1942 death of José Díaz at a Los Angeles County reservoir. Released after the California District Court of Appeal reversed all convictions in October 1944.

cases.charges: Second-degree murder of José Díaz
cases.convicted: Yes — conviction reversed in People v. Zammora (1944)

name: Manuel Delgado
description: Mexican-American youth and one of the twelve defendants convicted of murder or assault in the January 1943 Sleepy Lagoon mass trial, following the August 1942 death of José Díaz in Los Angeles County. The Sleepy Lagoon Defense Committee, with support from Hollywood progressives and the CIO, organized a national appeal. Convictions reversed October 1944 by the California District Court of Appeal.

cases.charges: Murder of José Díaz
cases.convicted: Yes — conviction reversed in People v. Zammora (1944)

name: Dalton Trumbo
description: Screenwriter and novelist (Johnny Got His Gun) who was one of the highest-paid writers in Hollywood before being subpoenaed by HUAC in October 1947. Refused to answer the committee's questions about Communist Party membership and was cited for contempt of Congress. Served eleven months in federal prison and worked under pseudonyms for years; he was credited for The Brave One under a front and finally re-credited for Spartacus and Exodus in 1960, effectively breaking the blacklist.

cases.charges: Contempt of Congress for refusing to answer HUAC

name: Albert Maltz
description: Screenwriter, playwright, and novelist (Pride of the Marines, This Gun for Hire). Subpoenaed by HUAC in October 1947 and one of ten unfriendly witnesses who refused to answer questions about Communist Party affiliation. Convicted of contempt of Congress and served twelve months in federal prison; blacklisted for nearly twenty years.

cases.charges: Contempt of Congress for refusing to answer HUAC

name: Lester Cole
description: Screenwriter and a founder of the Screen Writers Guild. Subpoenaed in October 1947 as one of the Hollywood Ten unfriendly witnesses before HUAC. Convicted of contempt of Congress, sentenced to one year in federal prison, and blacklisted for nearly two decades.

cases.charges: Contempt of Congress for refusing to answer HUAC

name: Edward Dmytryk
description: Film director (Murder, My Sweet; Crossfire) who was subpoenaed by HUAC in October 1947 as one of the Hollywood Ten. Initially refused to answer and served six months in federal prison for contempt of Congress. In 1951 he became the only member of the Ten to recant, naming names before HUAC and resuming his Hollywood career — a decision that made him a deeply controversial figure on the left.

cases.charges: Contempt of Congress for refusing to answer HUAC

name: Herbert Biberman
description: Film director and producer subpoenaed by HUAC in October 1947 as one of the Hollywood Ten. Convicted of contempt of Congress and served six months in federal prison. After the blacklist he directed Salt of the Earth (1954), an independent film about a New Mexico zinc miners' strike that was suppressed in the United States but later recognized as a classic of American leftist cinema.

cases.charges: Contempt of Congress for refusing to answer HUAC

name: Ring Lardner Jr.
description: Oscar-winning screenwriter (Woman of the Year, 1942) and son of humorist Ring Lardner. Subpoenaed by HUAC in October 1947 and famously answered, 'I could answer it, but if I did I would hate myself in the morning.' Convicted of contempt of Congress, served twelve months in federal prison, and was blacklisted for over a decade before winning a second Academy Award for M*A*S*H in 1971.

cases.charges: Contempt of Congress for refusing to answer HUAC

name: John Howard Lawson
description: Playwright, screenwriter, and founding president of the Screen Writers Guild, widely regarded as the leading Communist Party figure in Hollywood. The first of the Hollywood Ten unfriendly witnesses called before HUAC in October 1947, his hostile exchange with chairman J. Parnell Thomas set the tone for the hearings. Convicted of contempt of Congress and sentenced to twelve months in federal prison.

cases.charges: Contempt of Congress for refusing to answer HUAC

name: Alvah Bessie
description: Novelist, screenwriter (Objective, Burma!), and veteran of the Abraham Lincoln Brigade who fought against fascism in the Spanish Civil War. Subpoenaed by HUAC in October 1947 as one of the Hollywood Ten. Convicted of contempt of Congress, served ten months in federal prison, and was blacklisted for the remainder of his Hollywood career.

cases.charges: Contempt of Congress for refusing to answer HUAC

name: Samuel Ornitz
description: Novelist (Haunch, Paunch and Jowl) and screenwriter, the oldest member of the Hollywood Ten. Subpoenaed by HUAC in October 1947 for his Communist Party affiliations. Convicted of contempt of Congress and sentenced to twelve months in federal prison; suffered from cancer and was released early on health grounds.

cases.charges: Contempt of Congress for refusing to answer HUAC

name: Adrian Scott
description: Film producer (Crossfire, Murder My Sweet) and Hollywood Ten defendant. Subpoenaed by HUAC in October 1947 and convicted of contempt of Congress for refusing to answer questions about Communist Party membership. Served twelve months in federal prison and was blacklisted for the rest of his career, working under pseudonyms when he could find work at all.

cases.charges: Contempt of Congress for refusing to answer HUAC

name: Fred Korematsu
description: Japanese American who refused to comply with the May 1942 forced removal order from his Oakland, California home, arguing that Executive Order 9066 violated his constitutional rights. Arrested in May 1942 and convicted of violating Civilian Exclusion Order No. 34. His name is attached to the infamous Korematsu v. United States (1944) Supreme Court decision upholding the internment; his conviction was vacated by a federal court in 1983 on coram nobis review after the discovery of government suppression of evidence, and he was awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom in 1998.

cases.institution_name: Tanforan Assembly Center / Topaz War Relocation Center
cases.charges: Violation of Civilian Exclusion Order No. 34 under Executive Order 9066
cases.convicted: Yes — conviction vacated on coram nobis review in 1983
cases.sentence: 5 years probation; sent to Topaz internment camp

name: Gordon Hirabayashi
description: University of Washington student and Quaker conscientious objector who in May 1942 refused to comply with the curfew and forced removal orders applied to Japanese Americans on the West Coast. Convicted of curfew and exclusion order violations; his case produced the Supreme Court's Hirabayashi v. United States (1943) decision upholding the curfew. Convictions vacated by federal courts in the 1980s through coram nobis proceedings; awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom posthumously in 2012.

cases.charges: Violation of curfew and exclusion orders under Executive Order 9066
cases.convicted: Yes — convictions vacated on coram nobis review (1986-1987)
cases.sentence: 90 days for curfew/exclusion violations; additional time for draft-related refusal

name: Minoru Yasui
description: Oregon-born attorney and U.S. Army reserve officer who deliberately violated the military curfew imposed on Japanese Americans in March 1942 to create a test case. Convicted in federal court in Portland and sentenced to one year in prison; held for nine months in solitary confinement at the Multnomah County Jail before being sent to the Minidoka internment camp. The Supreme Court upheld his conviction in Yasui v. United States (1943); it was vacated in 1984 on coram nobis review. Awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom posthumously in 2015.

cases.charges: Violation of military curfew under Public Proclamation No. 3
cases.convicted: Yes — conviction vacated on coram nobis review in 1984
cases.sentence: 1 year federal imprisonment (served ~9 months in solitary); later sent to Minidoka internment camp

name: Mitsuye Endo
description: California state employee unjustly held at the Tule Lake and Topaz internment camps after being dismissed from her civil service job because of her Japanese ancestry. Her habeas corpus petition produced Ex parte Endo (1944), in which a unanimous Supreme Court ruled the War Relocation Authority could not detain a concededly loyal U.S. citizen — a decision announced the day after Korematsu and which effectively ended the internment camps.

cases.institution_name: Tule Lake / Topaz War Relocation Center
cases.charges: None — detained without charge under Executive Order 9066
cases.convicted: No — detained as a loyal citizen; SCOTUS ordered release in Ex parte Endo (1944)
cases.sentence: Indefinite administrative detention in WRA camps

name: Earl Browder
description: General Secretary of the Communist Party USA from 1934 to 1945 and the party's presidential candidate in 1936 and 1940. Convicted in January 1940 of passport fraud for traveling abroad in the 1920s using the alias 'Nicholas Dozenberg.' Sentenced to four years in federal prison and began serving in March 1941 at the U.S. Penitentiary in Atlanta. President Franklin D. Roosevelt commuted his sentence in May 1942 as a goodwill gesture toward the wartime Soviet ally.

cases.charges: Passport fraud (using the alias Nicholas Dozenberg)
cases.convicted: Yes — sentence commuted by President Franklin D. Roosevelt

name: Julius Rosenberg
description: American electrical engineer and Communist Party USA member convicted in 1951 of conspiracy to commit espionage for passing US atomic and military secrets to the Soviet Union during and after World War II. Along with his wife Ethel, he was the only American civilian executed for espionage-related activity during the Cold War. He maintained his innocence throughout but later evidence, including Venona decrypts and admissions from co-conspirators, confirmed his role running a Soviet spy ring.

cases.charges: Conspiracy to commit espionage (50 U.S.C. § 32(a))

name: Ethel Rosenberg
description: American homemaker and Communist Party member executed alongside her husband Julius for conspiracy to commit espionage in connection with passing atomic secrets to the Soviet Union. Her conviction rested heavily on testimony from her brother David Greenglass, who decades later admitted he had lied about her typing notes to protect his own wife. She was the first woman executed by the US federal government since Mary Surratt in 1865 and her case remains one of the most contested prosecutions of the McCarthy era.

cases.charges: Conspiracy to commit espionage (50 U.S.C. § 32(a))

name: Morton Sobell
description: American electrical engineer tried alongside the Rosenbergs in 1951 and convicted of conspiracy to commit espionage. He fled to Mexico shortly before his arrest but was abducted and returned to US custody. He served more than 17 years across several federal prisons, including Alcatraz, before his release in 1969; in 2008, at age 91, he publicly admitted he had spied for the Soviet Union, though he insisted his work involved military rather than atomic secrets.

cases.charges: Conspiracy to commit espionage

name: Harry Gold
description: American laboratory chemist who served as the principal courier between Soviet intelligence and atomic spy Klaus Fuchs at Los Alamos, and who also collected information from David Greenglass. Arrested in May 1950 after Fuchs identified him, he confessed within weeks and became a key witness against the Rosenbergs and Greenglass. He was sentenced to 30 years and paroled in 1965 after serving roughly half his sentence.

cases.charges: Espionage (18 U.S.C. § 794)

name: David Greenglass
description: US Army machinist assigned to the Manhattan Project at Los Alamos who passed sketches and notes about implosion-lens designs to Soviet intelligence through his brother-in-law Julius Rosenberg. After his arrest in June 1950 he became the prosecution's central witness against Julius and his own sister Ethel Rosenberg, testimony he partially recanted in a 2001 interview, admitting he had lied to shield his wife Ruth. He served roughly 9.5 years of a 15-year sentence and lived under an assumed name after his release.

cases.charges: Conspiracy to commit espionage

name: Judith Coplon
description: Department of Justice political analyst arrested by the FBI in March 1949 while allegedly attempting to pass classified counterintelligence reports to Soviet engineer Valentin Gubitchev. She was convicted in two separate trials in 1949 and 1950, but both convictions were overturned on appeal — Learned Hand's Second Circuit ruling found the FBI had arrested her without a warrant and concealed wiretaps from the court. Charges lingered until they were finally dismissed by the government in 1967; Venona later confirmed she had been a Soviet source, but she never served prison time.

cases.charges: Espionage and conspiracy to commit espionage (both convictions later overturned)
cases.convicted: No — convicted twice (1949, 1950), both reversed on appeal; never imprisoned
cases.sentence: 10 years (DC trial) + 15 years (NY trial), both vacated

name: Alger Hiss
description: Senior State Department official who helped organize the United Nations and was accused by Whittaker Chambers in 1948 of having been a Soviet agent in the 1930s. The statute of limitations on espionage had run, so he was tried for perjury for denying the allegations to HUAC; his first trial ended in a hung jury, and he was convicted in January 1950. He served 44 months of a 5-year sentence at Lewisburg and spent the rest of his life maintaining his innocence, though most historians now accept the Venona and Soviet archival evidence implicating him.

cases.charges: Two counts of perjury (18 U.S.C. § 1621)

name: William Walter Remington
description: Commerce Department economist accused by Elizabeth Bentley of having been a Soviet source in the 1940s. Indicted in 1950 for perjury after denying CPUSA membership before a grand jury, his first conviction was reversed; he was retried, reconvicted in 1953, and sentenced to three years at the Lewisburg federal penitentiary. He was beaten to death by fellow inmates in November 1954, a killing his biographers attribute to the prisoners' belief that they were attacking a 'communist spy.'

He was indicted for perjury in December 1952 over his testimony before the McCarran Internal Security Subcommittee; the charges were dismissed by the federal courts in 1955 after years under indictment. He was never convicted of any crime but his US academic career was effectively destroyed and he later relocated to the University of Leeds.

name: Pedro Albizu Campos
description: Harvard-trained lawyer and president of the Puerto Rican Nationalist Party who became the dominant figure in 20th-century Puerto Rican independence politics. He served roughly six years in the federal penitentiary in Atlanta (1937–1943) for seditious conspiracy after the 1936 Ponce massacre, then was rearrested in 1950 following the Jayuya Uprising and the Blair House attack and sentenced to 80 years. Held mostly at La Princesa in San Juan, he suffered radiation burns he attributed to deliberate experimentation by US authorities, was briefly pardoned in 1953, reimprisoned in 1954, and pardoned again in 1964 by Governor Sánchez Vilella when he was dying.

name: Oscar Collazo
description: Puerto Rican Nationalist who, with Griselio Torresola, attempted to assassinate President Harry S. Truman at Blair House on November 1, 1950, during the Jayuya Uprising. Wounded in the gunfight that killed Torresola and White House police officer Leslie Coffelt, he was convicted and sentenced to death, but Truman commuted the sentence to life in 1952. After 29 years in federal prison, mostly at Leavenworth, he was pardoned by President Jimmy Carter in September 1979 and returned to Puerto Rico a hero of the independence movement.

name: Griselio Torresola
description: Puerto Rican Nationalist from Jayuya who, with Oscar Collazo, carried out the November 1, 1950 armed attack on Blair House intending to assassinate President Harry S. Truman during the Nationalist uprising on the island. He killed White House police officer Leslie Coffelt before being shot dead by Coffelt's return fire on the steps of Blair House. He was never imprisoned but is commemorated alongside Collazo as a martyr of the Puerto Rican independence movement.

name: Lolita Lebrón
description: Puerto Rican Nationalist leader who on March 1, 1954 led an armed attack on the US House of Representatives, unfurling a Puerto Rican flag from the visitors' gallery and shouting '¡Viva Puerto Rico libre!' as she and three comrades opened fire, wounding five congressmen. She received a 50-year sentence and served 25 years, most of them at the Federal Reformatory for Women in Alderson, West Virginia, before being pardoned by President Jimmy Carter in September 1979. She returned to Puerto Rico an icon of the independence movement and continued activism until her death in 2010.

cases.charges: Assault with intent to kill; seditious conspiracy

name: Rafael Cancel Miranda
description: Puerto Rican Nationalist who, alongside Lolita Lebrón, Andrés Figueroa Cordero, and Irvin Flores, opened fire from the visitors' gallery of the US House of Representatives on March 1, 1954, wounding five congressmen. Convicted of assault with intent to kill and seditious conspiracy, he served 25 years in federal prisons including Leavenworth and Marion before being pardoned by President Jimmy Carter in September 1979. He returned to Puerto Rico and remained a leading voice for independence until his death in 2020.

cases.charges: Assault with intent to kill; seditious conspiracy

name: Andrés Figueroa Cordero

cases.charges: Attempted murder of police officers; parole violation

name: David Hilliard
description: Chief of Staff of the Black Panther Party during its peak years. Hilliard was prosecuted under federal law for allegedly threatening the life of President Richard Nixon in a November 1969 anti-war speech in San Francisco. He later served time on assault charges from the 1968 Oakland shootout and went on to lead the Huey P. Newton Foundation.

cases.institution_name: Federal court — Northern District of California
cases.charges: Threatening the life of the President of the United States (18 U.S.C. § 871)

name: Elmer Geronimo Pratt
description: Decorated Vietnam paratrooper who became deputy minister of defense for the Los Angeles chapter of the Black Panther Party. He was framed for the 1968 tennis-court murder of Caroline Olsen in Santa Monica and spent 27 years in California prisons, including eight in solitary, before his conviction was vacated in 1997 on grounds that prosecutors hid that key witness Julius Butler was an FBI/LAPD informant. He won a $4.5 million civil-rights settlement in 2000.

cases.charges: First-degree murder; kidnapping; robbery (Caroline Olsen killing)

name: Fred Hampton
description: Chairman of the Illinois chapter of the Black Panther Party and architect of the original Rainbow Coalition uniting the Panthers, the Young Lords and the Young Patriots. He was shot to death in his bed in a pre-dawn raid by the Cook County State's Attorney's tactical unit, Chicago Police and the FBI on December 4, 1969. A 1982 civil settlement of $1.85 million was paid by federal, county and city governments to survivors and the families of Hampton and Mark Clark.

name: Mark Clark
description: Captain of defense in the Peoria, Illinois chapter of the Black Panther Party. He was 22 years old when he was killed alongside Illinois BPP chairman Fred Hampton in the police/FBI raid on Hampton's West Side Chicago apartment on December 4, 1969. A single shot from Clark's shotgun — fired as he was dying — was the only round fired by anyone in the apartment.

name: Alprentice Bunchy Carter
description: Founder of the Southern California chapter of the Black Panther Party and the chapter's deputy minister of defense. A former Slauson gang leader turned revolutionary while incarcerated at Soledad, he was shot to death on the UCLA campus on January 17, 1969, along with John Huggins, in a confrontation with members of Ron Karenga's US Organization that the FBI's COINTELPRO program had actively worked to provoke.

name: Afeni Shakur
description: New York Black Panther Party member and one of the Panther 21 defendants indicted in April 1969 on conspiracy charges to bomb department stores, police stations and the New York Botanical Garden. She conducted much of her own defense while pregnant and was acquitted along with all of her co-defendants on May 13, 1971. One month later she gave birth to her son, the future rapper Tupac Shakur.

cases.charges: Conspiracy to bomb police stations, department stores and the New York Botanical Garden

name: Lumumba Shakur
description: Lead defendant of the Panther 21, the New York Black Panther Party members indicted in April 1969 on a 156-count conspiracy indictment. After an eight-month trial — the longest in New York State history at the time — the jury acquitted him and all co-defendants on every count on May 13, 1971. He later relocated to the South and died in 1986.

cases.charges: Conspiracy to bomb police stations, department stores and the New York Botanical Garden

name: Dhoruba bin Wahad
description: A leader of the Bronx chapter of the Black Panther Party and a defendant in the Panther 21 conspiracy case, in which all defendants were acquitted in 1971. He was later convicted in the May 1971 machine-gun ambush of two NYPD officers; he served 19 years before the conviction was vacated in 1990 after disclosure of suppressed FBI COINTELPRO documents. He won a civil settlement against the FBI and NYPD.

cases.charges: Attempted murder of two NYPD officers (Curry and Binetti ambush)

name: Joan Bird
description: Bronx Community College nursing student and Black Panther Party member who was 19 when arrested in January 1969 as part of the Panther 21 case. She testified that she had been beaten and hung upside down out of a window by NYPD interrogators. She was acquitted with her co-defendants on May 13, 1971 after roughly two years in pre-trial detention.

cases.charges: Conspiracy to bomb police stations, department stores and the New York Botanical Garden

name: Michael Cetewayo Tabor
description: Harlem Black Panther Party member, Panther 21 defendant and author of the influential 1970 pamphlet Capitalism Plus Dope Equals Genocide. While free on bail during the Panther 21 trial in early 1971, he jumped bail with co-defendant Richard Moore amid the BPP's east-west split and fled to Algeria. He later settled in Zambia, where he lived until his death in 2010.

cases.charges: Conspiracy to bomb police stations, department stores and the New York Botanical Garden
cases.convicted: Jumped bail; charges ultimately dismissed after co-defendants' acquittal

name: Angela Davis
description: Philosopher, Communist Party USA member and UCLA professor who was fired by the Reagan-led University of California Board of Regents in 1969 for her political affiliations. She was charged with aggravated kidnapping, murder and conspiracy after firearms registered to her were used by Jonathan Jackson in the August 7, 1970 Marin County courthouse takeover. After 16 months in jail and placement on the FBI's Ten Most Wanted list, she was acquitted on all counts by an all-white jury on June 4, 1972.

cases.charges: Aggravated kidnapping; first-degree murder; conspiracy (Marin County courthouse incident)

name: George Jackson
description: Author of Soledad Brother: The Prison Letters of George Jackson and Blood in My Eye, and a field marshal of the Black Panther Party. Sentenced in 1961 to one year to life for a $70 gas-station robbery, he was charged with the January 1970 killing of Soledad guard John Mills along with co-defendants Drumgo and Clutchette — the Soledad Brothers case. He was shot dead at San Quentin on August 21, 1971 in what authorities called an escape attempt but supporters maintain was an assassination.

cases.charges: Murder of correctional officer John Mills
cases.sentence: One year to life (original 1961 robbery sentence)

name: Fleeta Drumgo
description: One of the three Soledad Brothers charged with the January 16, 1970 killing of Soledad Correctional Officer John Mills in retaliation for the fatal shooting of three Black prisoners by a guard three days earlier. He and co-defendant John Clutchette were acquitted on March 27, 1972 after George Jackson had been killed at San Quentin. Drumgo was murdered in Oakland in 1979.

cases.charges: Murder of correctional officer John Mills

name: John Clutchette
description: The third of the Soledad Brothers, charged with co-defendants George Jackson and Fleeta Drumgo with the January 16, 1970 killing of Soledad guard John Mills. He was acquitted on March 27, 1972. After his release he was later convicted on an unrelated 1980 California murder charge and has been in custody since.

cases.charges: Murder of correctional officer John Mills

name: Hugo Yogi Pinell
description: Nicaraguan-born revolutionary and the only member of the San Quentin Six convicted of aggravated assault on guards during the August 21, 1971 events surrounding George Jackson's death. He spent more than four decades in solitary confinement — among the longest in U.S. history — before being moved to general population, where he was killed by other prisoners at California State Prison-Sacramento on August 12, 2015.

cases.charges: Conspiracy; aggravated assault on correctional officers (August 21, 1971 events)
cases.convicted: Yes — convicted of aggravated assault; acquitted of murder

name: Willie Tate
description: One of the San Quentin Six, charged along with five others with conspiracy and murder in connection with the August 21, 1971 deaths of three guards and three prisoners at San Quentin during the killing of George Jackson. After a 17-month trial — the longest and most expensive in California history to that point — he was acquitted on all counts on August 12, 1976.

cases.charges: Conspiracy; murder (August 21, 1971 events)

name: David Johnson
description: One of the San Quentin Six, charged with conspiracy and murder following the August 21, 1971 events at San Quentin in which George Jackson and five others died. He was acquitted of the most serious charges in the August 1976 verdict that ended the longest criminal trial in California history.

cases.charges: Conspiracy; murder (August 21, 1971 events)

name: Luis Talamantez
description: Chicano member of the San Quentin Six who endured five years in pretrial isolation before going to trial with his co-defendants. Acquitted on all charges on August 12, 1976, he went on to become a leading prison-abolition organizer with the Prisoners' Rights Union and California Prison Focus.

cases.charges: Conspiracy; murder (August 21, 1971 events)

name: Johnny Spain
description: The only member of the San Quentin Six convicted of murder for the August 21, 1971 deaths at San Quentin. A Black Panther who had become close to George Jackson, he was convicted in 1976 but the conviction was overturned in 1988 by a federal court that found the trial judge had unconstitutionally chained Spain through the proceedings. He was paroled in 1988.

cases.charges: Conspiracy; murder (August 21, 1971 events)
cases.convicted: Yes — conviction overturned 1988 on shackling violation

name: Dennis Banks
description: Ojibwe activist who co-founded the American Indian Movement in Minneapolis in 1968. He helped lead the 71-day occupation of Wounded Knee on the Pine Ridge Reservation from February 27 to May 8, 1973, and was convicted in 1975 in the Custer County, South Dakota courthouse riot case. He fled rather than be sentenced and lived under sanctuary granted by California Governor Jerry Brown from 1976 until 1984, when he surrendered and served 14 months in South Dakota.

cases.charges: Riot; assault without intent to kill (Custer County courthouse, 1973)

name: Russell Means
description: Oglala Lakota activist and one of the most visible leaders of the American Indian Movement. He helped lead the November 1972 takeover of the Bureau of Indian Affairs building in Washington and the 1973 occupation of Wounded Knee, and faced multiple federal charges arising from those actions. The Wounded Knee Leadership Trial ended in September 1974 when a federal judge dismissed all charges against Means and Banks for government misconduct.

cases.institution_name: Federal court — District of South Dakota
cases.charges: Conspiracy; assault on federal officers; theft; arson (Wounded Knee occupation)
cases.convicted: No — case dismissed for prosecutorial misconduct

name: Carter Camp
description: Ponca leader of the American Indian Movement and the national AIM officer who led the initial seizure of Wounded Knee on February 27, 1973. He was convicted in 1975 of assault on a federal officer at Wounded Knee and served two years in federal prison. He remained active in Indigenous sovereignty work until his death in 2013.

cases.charges: Assault on a United States Marshal at Wounded Knee

name: Pedro Bissonette
description: Oglala Lakota co-founder of the Oglala Sioux Civil Rights Organization (OSCRO) and one of the primary organizers of the Wounded Knee occupation in 1973. After surrendering and facing federal charges arising from Wounded Knee, he was shot and killed by BIA tribal police on the Pine Ridge Reservation on October 17, 1973, in circumstances that AIM and his family have always disputed.

name: Leonard Crow Dog
description: Sicangu Lakota medicine man and spiritual leader of the American Indian Movement who restored the Ghost Dance during the 1973 occupation of Wounded Knee. He was convicted in 1975 on multiple charges arising from Wounded Knee and a later incident, and originally sentenced to 13 years. After an international campaign by Amnesty International, William Kunstler and others, his sentence was reduced and he was paroled in 1977.

cases.charges: Interfering with federal officers; assault; possession of firearms (Wounded Knee and related)
cases.convicted: Yes — 13-year sentence reduced after international campaign

name: Imari Obadele
description: Co-founder and president of the Provisional Government of the Republic of New Afrika, which sought to establish an independent Black-majority nation in five Deep South states. He was the lead defendant in the prosecution of the RNA-11 after the August 18, 1971 FBI/Jackson Police raid on RNA headquarters in Jackson, Mississippi, in which officer William Skinner was killed. Sentenced to twelve years in federal prison on a separate conspiracy charge, he was released in 1980 and later earned a Ph.D.

cases.charges: Conspiracy to assault federal officers (RNA-11 case)

name: Hekima Ana
description: Citizen of the Republic of New Afrika and one of the RNA-11 charged in the aftermath of the August 18, 1971 FBI and Jackson Police raid on RNA headquarters at 1148 Lewis Street in Jackson, Mississippi. He was convicted in Mississippi state court of murder in the death of Jackson Police Lt. William Skinner during the raid and served a lengthy prison term.

cases.charges: Murder of Jackson Police Lt. William Skinner (1971 RNA raid)

name: Tarik Ali
description: Citizen of the Republic of New Afrika and one of the RNA-11 charged after the August 18, 1971 FBI/Jackson Police raid on RNA national headquarters in Jackson, Mississippi. He served prison time in Mississippi on charges arising from the gunfight in which Jackson Police Lt. William Skinner was killed and an FBI agent was wounded.

cases.charges: Assault; conspiracy (RNA-11 case)

name: Bernardine Dohrn
description: Former leader of the Students for a Democratic Society national office who co-founded the Weather Underground Organization in 1969 and went underground in 1970. She remained a federal fugitive for over a decade; most federal charges against her were dismissed in 1973 after the Supreme Court's Keith decision exposed illegal government surveillance. She surfaced in 1980, served brief time on a contempt charge, and later directed the Children and Family Justice Center at Northwestern University Law School.

cases.institution_name: Federal court — Northern District of Illinois
cases.charges: Federal anti-riot and conspiracy charges (Days of Rage); contempt of grand jury
cases.convicted: Federal charges dismissed 1973; later served brief contempt time

name: Bill Ayers
description: Founder and central organizer of the Weather Underground Organization who went underground in 1970 after the Greenwich Village townhouse explosion killed three comrades. He remained a fugitive until 1980, when he and Bernardine Dohrn surfaced; federal charges against him had been dismissed in 1973 because of unconstitutional FBI surveillance. He later became a Distinguished Professor of Education at the University of Illinois at Chicago.

cases.institution_name: Federal court — Eastern District of Michigan
cases.charges: Federal conspiracy; bombing-related charges
cases.convicted: No — charges dismissed 1973 for illegal government surveillance

name: Mark Rudd
description: Chairman of the Columbia University chapter of Students for a Democratic Society who led the 1968 occupation of Columbia. He helped found the Weather Underground in 1969 and went underground after the March 1970 Greenwich Village townhouse explosion. He surfaced in 1977 and pleaded guilty to misdemeanor charges, receiving a fine and probation; he later taught math at a community college in New Mexico.

cases.charges: Criminal trespass and related misdemeanors (Columbia occupation and surrender)

name: Cathlyn Wilkerson
description: Weather Underground member who survived the March 6, 1970 Greenwich Village townhouse explosion at her father's home, in which three Weather members died while assembling a bomb. She remained a federal fugitive until 1980; after surrendering she served 11 months on charges related to the explosion and possession of explosives. She later worked as a math teacher in New York City.

cases.charges: Possession of explosives (Greenwich Village townhouse)

name: David Gilbert
description: Former Columbia SDS activist and Weather Underground member who later joined the May 19th Communist Organization. He was convicted of felony murder for his role as a getaway driver in the October 20, 1981 Brink's armored-car robbery in Nyack, New York, in which two police officers and a Brink's guard were killed. Sentenced to 75 years to life, he served 40 years before Governor Andrew Cuomo commuted his sentence in 2021; he was paroled that November.

cases.charges: Felony murder; armed robbery (Brink's robbery, Nyack NY)
cases.sentence: 75 years to life (commuted by Gov. Cuomo 2021)

name: Judith Clark
description: Weather Underground and May 19th Communist Organization member convicted of felony murder for her role as a getaway driver in the October 20, 1981 Brink's robbery in Nyack, New York. After refusing to participate in her own trial, she was sentenced to 75 years to life. In prison she became a hospice volunteer, AIDS counselor and college program leader; Governor Andrew Cuomo commuted her sentence in 2016 and she was paroled in April 2019 after 38 years.

cases.charges: Felony murder; armed robbery (Brink's robbery, Nyack NY)
cases.sentence: 75 years to life (commuted by Gov. Cuomo 2016)

name: Kathy Boudin
description: Weather Underground member and Greenwich Village townhouse explosion survivor who went underground for a decade. She was arrested and convicted of felony murder for her role in the October 20, 1981 Brink's robbery in Nyack, New York. She pleaded guilty to murder and robbery and was sentenced to 20 years to life; paroled in 2003 after 22 years, she co-founded the Center for Justice at Columbia University.

cases.charges: Felony murder; armed robbery (Brink's robbery, Nyack NY)

name: Susan Rosenberg
description: Member of the May 19th Communist Organization who went underground after being named in the Brink's robbery investigation. She was arrested in New Jersey in 1984 with co-defendant Timothy Blunk while unloading 740 pounds of dynamite and weapons from a storage locker, and sentenced to 58 years in federal prison. President Bill Clinton commuted her sentence on his last day in office, January 20, 2001, after she had served 16 years.

cases.charges: Possession of unregistered firearms and explosives

name: Marilyn Buck
description: White anti-imperialist who worked closely with the Black Liberation Army and May 19th Communist Organization. She was convicted of participating in the November 1979 escape of Assata Shakur from Clinton Correctional Facility for Women and the October 20, 1981 Brink's robbery in Nyack, New York, and received an 80-year federal sentence. A poet whose work won a PEN award, she was released on parole on July 15, 2010 and died of cancer 20 days later.

cases.charges: RICO; armed robbery; aiding escape (Assata Shakur escape, Brink's robbery)

name: Linda Evans
description: Weather Underground and May 19th Communist Organization member arrested in 1985 and convicted of harboring a fugitive and using false identification to buy firearms, then again in the 1988 Resistance Conspiracy case for a series of 1983–85 bombings of U.S. government buildings, including the U.S. Capitol. She received a 40-year sentence; President Bill Clinton commuted her sentence on January 20, 2001, after she had served 16 years.

cases.charges: Conspiracy to bomb federal buildings (Resistance Conspiracy case); firearms violations

name: Laura Whitehorn
description: Weather Underground and May 19th Communist Organization member convicted in the 1988 Resistance Conspiracy case for a series of 1983–85 bombings of U.S. government and military targets, including the November 7, 1983 bombing of the U.S. Capitol in response to the invasion of Grenada. She pleaded guilty and was sentenced to 20 years; she was released in August 1999 after more than 14 years inside, including long stretches in the federal control unit at Lexington.

cases.charges: Conspiracy to bomb federal buildings (Resistance Conspiracy case)

name: Benjamin Chavis
description: United Church of Christ minister and civil rights organizer who led the Commission for Racial Justice's work in Wilmington, North Carolina, after the 1971 firebombing of Mike's Grocery during a period of school-desegregation violence. He was the most prominent of the Wilmington 10, sentenced to 29-34 years on conspiracy and arson charges. The convictions were overturned by the Fourth Circuit in 1980, and Governor Bev Perdue granted full pardons of innocence to all ten on December 31, 2012.
affiliation: United Church of Christ Commission for Racial Justice

cases.charges: Conspiracy; arson (Mike's Grocery firebombing, Wilmington NC)

name: Connie Tindall
description: One of the Wilmington 10, convicted in 1972 on conspiracy and arson charges related to the February 1971 firebombing of Mike's Grocery in Wilmington, North Carolina. After serving years in North Carolina prisons, his conviction was vacated in 1980. Governor Bev Perdue issued posthumous and living pardons of innocence to all ten defendants on December 31, 2012.

cases.charges: Conspiracy; arson (Wilmington NC firebombing)

name: Marvin Patrick
description: One of the Wilmington 10, convicted in 1972 of conspiracy and arson in connection with the February 1971 firebombing of Mike's Grocery during the civil-rights school-desegregation crisis in Wilmington, North Carolina. The convictions of the group were vacated by the Fourth Circuit in 1980, and on December 31, 2012 Governor Bev Perdue granted pardons of innocence to all ten.

cases.charges: Conspiracy; arson (Wilmington NC firebombing)

name: Wayne Moore
description: One of the Wilmington 10, convicted in 1972 of conspiracy and arson stemming from the February 1971 firebombing of Mike's Grocery in Wilmington, North Carolina. After international human-rights campaigns including Amnesty International's recognition of the ten as prisoners of conscience, the convictions were vacated in 1980 and Governor Bev Perdue issued pardons of innocence in 2012.

cases.charges: Conspiracy; arson (Wilmington NC firebombing)

name: Reginald Epps
description: One of the Wilmington 10, convicted in 1972 of conspiracy and arson tied to the firebombing of Mike's Grocery in Wilmington, North Carolina. He served the shortest of the ten sentences and was released earliest. The Fourth Circuit overturned the convictions in 1980 and Governor Bev Perdue granted pardons of innocence to all ten defendants on December 31, 2012.

cases.charges: Conspiracy; arson (Wilmington NC firebombing)

name: Jerry Jacobs
description: One of the Wilmington 10, convicted in 1972 of conspiracy and arson in connection with the February 1971 firebombing in Wilmington, North Carolina. The Fourth Circuit Court of Appeals overturned the convictions in 1980 after key prosecution witnesses recanted. Governor Bev Perdue granted pardons of innocence to the entire Wilmington 10 on December 31, 2012.

cases.charges: Conspiracy; arson (Wilmington NC firebombing)

name: James McKoy
description: One of the Wilmington 10, convicted in 1972 on conspiracy and arson charges arising from the February 1971 firebombing of Mike's Grocery in Wilmington, North Carolina. Convictions of all ten were vacated by the Fourth Circuit in 1980 after recantations by prosecution witnesses, and Governor Bev Perdue granted pardons of innocence on December 31, 2012.

cases.charges: Conspiracy; arson (Wilmington NC firebombing)

name: Willie Earl Vereen
description: One of the Wilmington 10, convicted in 1972 of conspiracy and arson in connection with the February 1971 firebombing in Wilmington, North Carolina. Their convictions, which made the ten internationally recognized political prisoners, were vacated in 1980; pardons of innocence followed on December 31, 2012 from Governor Bev Perdue.

cases.charges: Conspiracy; arson (Wilmington NC firebombing)

name: William Wright Jr.
description: One of the Wilmington 10, convicted in 1972 of conspiracy and arson in connection with the February 1971 firebombing of Mike's Grocery in Wilmington, North Carolina. The Fourth Circuit vacated the convictions in 1980 after prosecution witnesses recanted; Governor Bev Perdue granted pardons of innocence to all ten Wilmington defendants on December 31, 2012.

cases.charges: Conspiracy; arson (Wilmington NC firebombing)

name: Anne Sheppard Turner
description: The only white defendant and only woman among the Wilmington 10, a white anti-poverty worker convicted along with nine Black men of conspiracy and arson tied to the 1971 Wilmington firebombing. She received the shortest sentence of the group. Convictions were vacated in 1980 and Governor Bev Perdue granted pardons of innocence to all ten on December 31, 2012.

cases.institution_name: North Carolina Correctional Center for Women
cases.charges: Conspiracy; arson (Wilmington NC firebombing)

name: Karleton Armstrong
description: Anti-Vietnam War activist and member of the New Year's Gang who, with three others, bombed the Army Mathematics Research Center in Sterling Hall on the University of Wisconsin–Madison campus in the early hours of August 24, 1970. The blast killed physics postdoctoral researcher Robert Fassnacht. After fleeing to Canada, Armstrong was extradited and surrendered in 1972, pleaded guilty to second-degree murder and arson and served eight years.

cases.charges: Second-degree murder; arson; conspiracy (Sterling Hall bombing)

name: Dwight Armstrong
description: Younger brother of Karleton Armstrong and member of the New Year's Gang of four who carried out the August 24, 1970 Sterling Hall bombing at the University of Wisconsin–Madison, which destroyed the Army Mathematics Research Center and killed researcher Robert Fassnacht. After seven years on the run, he surrendered in Toronto in 1977, pleaded guilty, and served three years of a seven-year sentence.

cases.charges: Conspiracy; arson; destruction of government property (Sterling Hall bombing)

name: David Fine
description: Member of the New Year's Gang of four who bombed the Sterling Hall Army Mathematics Research Center at the University of Wisconsin–Madison on August 24, 1970, killing physics researcher Robert Fassnacht. The youngest of the bombers, he was 18 at the time. He surrendered in San Rafael, California in January 1976, pleaded guilty and served three years of a seven-year sentence.

cases.charges: Conspiracy; destruction of federal property (Sterling Hall bombing)

name: Russell Little
description: Early member of the Symbionese Liberation Army who, with Joseph Remiro, was convicted of the November 6, 1973 assassination of Oakland Schools Superintendent Marcus Foster — the SLA's first action. The conviction was reversed on appeal in 1981 because of an error in jury instructions; on retrial in 1981 a jury acquitted him on all charges.

cases.charges: First-degree murder (assassination of Oakland Schools Supt. Marcus Foster)

name: Joseph Remiro
description: Vietnam War veteran and Symbionese Liberation Army member convicted with Russell Little of the November 6, 1973 assassination of Oakland Schools Superintendent Marcus Foster. Sentenced to life in 1975, he has remained incarcerated; Little's conviction was overturned but Remiro's was not. He is among the longest-held political prisoners in California.

cases.charges: First-degree murder (assassination of Oakland Schools Supt. Marcus Foster)

name: Emily Harris
description: Member of the Symbionese Liberation Army who, with her then-husband Bill Harris, participated in the February 1974 kidnapping of newspaper heiress Patricia Hearst. Convicted in 1976 of the kidnapping, she served eight years. In 2001 she pleaded guilty in the 1975 Crocker National Bank robbery in Carmichael, California in which Myrna Opsahl was killed, and served an additional seven years.

cases.charges: Kidnapping; armed robbery (Hearst kidnapping); later second-degree murder (Opsahl killing)
cases.sentence: 11 years (served 8 on kidnapping); later 8 years (Opsahl plea)

name: Bill Harris
description: Vietnam veteran and Symbionese Liberation Army member who, with his then-wife Emily Harris, took part in the February 4, 1974 kidnapping of Patricia Hearst. Convicted in 1976 of kidnapping and robbery, he served eight years. He pleaded guilty in 2001 to second-degree murder in the 1975 Crocker National Bank robbery in which Myrna Opsahl was killed.

cases.charges: Kidnapping; armed robbery (Hearst kidnapping); later second-degree murder (Opsahl killing)
cases.sentence: 11 years (served 8 on kidnapping); later 8 years (Opsahl plea)

name: Reies Lopez Tijerina
description: Chicano land-grant activist who founded La Alianza Federal de Mercedes Libres to recover Spanish and Mexican land grants in New Mexico. On June 5, 1967 he led the armed raid on the Rio Arriba County courthouse at Tierra Amarilla, New Mexico, intended to free arrested Alianza members and make a citizen's arrest of the district attorney. He served two years in federal prison and one to three years in New Mexico state custody for the courthouse raid.

cases.charges: Assault on federal officers; destruction of federal property (Kit Carson National Forest occupation)
False imprisonment; assault (Tierra Amarilla courthouse raid)
